title: How a Forgotten Wire Turned a Cheap Chip Into a Brainlike Neuron
gist: Researchers Mario Lanza and Sebastian Pazos report that leaving a standard MOSFET's bulk terminal unconnected accidentally produces neuron-like integrate-and-fire spiking (and, in another mode, adjustable synaptic weights), letting a single cheap industry-standard transistor replace the dozens-to-hundreds of transistors today's neuromorphic neurons and synapses require, at 100% yield and CMOS-manufacturable.

insights.claim: Data-center GPUs consume as much as 1,000 watts each while running around the clock (comparable to a vacuum cleaner or stove), versus under 1W for a newer smartphone, quantifying the energy inefficiency neuromorphic computing targets.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: The 1kW-per-GPU baseline is the cost/energy pain point that any credible neuromorphic alternative is measured against; frames the TAM for brain-inspired silicon.
insights.quote: each of those GPUs achieves that by consuming as much as 1,000 watts apiece
insights.locator: AI requires processing massive amounts of data

insights.claim: The human brain is roughly one million times as energy efficient as current AI hardware at many comparable tasks, and state-of-the-art brain-inspired chips have already achieved up to thousandfold power reductions versus GPUs/CPUs on the same task.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: The million-times headroom and existing 1000x demonstrations define the upside if neuromorphic scales; a long-horizon disruption vector for GPU-centric AI compute.
insights.quote: The brain is roughly one million times as energy efficient at many of the comparable tasks we set for AI.
insights.locator: The brain is roughly one million times

insights.claim: The neuron behavior was discovered in 2024 when a student forgot to connect the bulk terminal of a transistor and observed a sudden nonlinear current increase that self-relaxed as voltage ramped down, a hysteresis loop resembling a biological neuron's action potential.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: A serendipitous lab error, not a designed device, underpins the claim; noteworthy because it means the effect exists in standard silicon already fabricated everywhere.
insights.quote: One day the student forgot to connect the bulk terminal of the transistor. What he observed was a sudden increase in current with high nonlinearity that self-relaxed when the voltage was ramped down

insights.claim: The MOSFETs exhibiting the effect were built on the 180-nanometer node (cutting-edge in 2000), showing the neuron behavior does not require advanced or expensive process technology.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: Using ancient, cheap 180nm silicon means neuromorphic devices could be made on fully depreciated fabs, a radically low-cost manufacturing path.
insights.locator: The MOSFETs were created in a commercial foundry

insights.claim: The neuron effect arises because a floating bulk terminal lets impact-ionization holes accumulate and raise the bulk voltage until a hidden bipolar-junction transistor inside every MOSFET triggers, producing a sharp current spike, exactly the integrate-and-fire behavior of a neuron.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: Because the mechanism is an intrinsic parasitic BJT present in all MOSFETs, the capability is universal to CMOS, not a special material; strengthens manufacturability claims.
insights.locator: It helps here to imagine a MOSFET as two different kinds

insights.claim: Adding a controlled bulk-terminal resistance (implemented via a second MOSFET) made all transistors fire at the same voltage with almost no variability, converting a flaky effect into reproducible neuron behavior.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: The two-transistor cell offering electronic control of firing is the practical engineering unlock; variability control is what makes it manufacturable at scale.
insights.quote: Simply setting that resistance suddenly caused all the transistors to fire at the same voltage with hardly any variability.

insights.claim: The same MOSFET can act as a synapse: at specific bulk-resistance values, impact-ionization charge trapped in the gate dielectric produces a stable, electronically adjustable conductance, with negative bulk-source voltage decreasing and positive increasing it.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: One device type serving as both neuron and synapse (together called NSRAM) means a whole neural fabric could be built from a single manufacturable primitive.
insights.locator: Recall that in ordinary operation some electrons

insights.claim: Every tested neuron device worked over 10 million cycles with zero failures, and the behavior was reproduced on chips from a different foundry with perfect yield, addressing the reliability problems that have plagued memristor-based neuromorphic approaches.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: 10M-cycle endurance and 100% cross-foundry yield are the metrics that distinguish this from two decades of failed experimental neuromorphic devices; the core investability signal.
insights.quote: every single one of the devices we tested worked over 10 million cycles. Not even one of them failed during our tests.
insights.locator: We had to make sure the phenomenon would last

insights.claim: The approach exhibits 100% yield in key figures of merit with near-zero variability and is fully compatible with today's silicon manufacturing lines, unlike memristor-like devices that consumed millions of dollars over 20 years with limited success.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: Directly challenges the memristor/experimental-device thesis that has dominated neuromorphic R&D funding; a potential re-rating of where neuromorphic value accrues.
insights.quote: We managed it in the cheapest and most industry-standard device: the MOSFET.
insights.locator: To be honest, we were amazed

themes.theme: Efficiency advantage and path to market

insights.claim: The single-MOSFET neuron/synapse can produce with one or two transistors the signals that today require dozens to hundreds of components, a dramatic area, cost, and performance advantage over CMOS-emulated neuromorphic circuits.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: A ~100x device-count reduction is the economic case; if it holds, it reshapes the cost structure of edge-AI neuromorphic chips.
insights.quote: We can produce with only one or two transistors the electronic signals that today require, at an industrial level, dozens and sometimes even hundreds of components.

insights.claim: The authors expect these chips to first fit smaller-scale edge-AI tasks (battery-powered systems) and only potentially compete with state-of-the-art GPUs if they can be scaled up, tempering near-term expectations.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: Realistic timeline: edge/battery applications first, datacenter GPU competition is a long-shot long-term; invest against edge-AI adoption, not imminent GPU displacement.
insights.quote: These chips will first be a fit for smaller-scale, “edge-AI” tasks, such as bringing greater intelligence to battery-powered systems.
insights.locator: But all that will be worthwhile

insights.claim: Realizing functional neuromorphic systems still requires better device models, circuit- and system-level simulation, peripheral driver circuitry, and multiple fabrication rounds, indicating meaningful engineering runway remains before products ship.
insights.speaker: IEEE Spectrum
insights.so_what: Signals the discovery is early-stage; commercialization is years out despite the strong device-level results.
insights.locator: Building functional circuits for brain-inspired computing
